select_when: A negotiation or partnership sounds like a price or policy dispute on the surface, but the real issue may be leverage, outside options, or commitment costs.
The team needs to know who can actually wait, walk away, redirect demand, or absorb reversal costs before accepting the stated framing of the deal.
A commitment, integration, or dependency may invert leverage over time even if the current terms look favorable at signature.
The next move should map fallback lanes, enforcement credibility, and the point where lock-in changes what is actually negotiable.
avoid_when: The true constraint is exogenous or non-negotiable, so treating the situation as a power contest would distract from the actual bottleneck.
The team is already over-reading symbolic status or tone as leverage and needs a factual capability or execution review instead.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model focused on outside options, credible walk-away, lock-in inversion, and hidden commitment costs rather than generic negotiation advice.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broad bargaining rhetoric because the raw source is stronger on leverage structure, reversibility, and dependency timing.
open_questions: The output type compresses a source that also spans multi-party weakest-link diagnosis and timing control.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit on hidden outside options, leverage inversion after commitment, and the difference between stated terms and real bargaining power.

avoid_when.note: The anti-activation conditions are grounded in source warnings against seeing every disagreement as a power contest or mistaking status for leverage.

input_type.note: The source clearly activates on leverage-structure problems, while the typed input remains a normalized operational label.

output_type.note: The source supports explicit mapping of fallback options, commitment gradients, and credible threats; the output label compresses that into a reusable artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Power dynamics is strongest as systems-aware leverage diagnosis rather than topical negotiation scripting.
